Children at Risk: Poverty, Disease & the Global Development Gap

A UNICEF Data Story on Child Deprivation and Healthcare Access

Author

Aman Singh Parihar A00050767

Published

today

from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')
Drive already mounted at /content/drive; to attempt to forcibly remount, call drive.mount("/content/drive", force_remount=True).
Code 
# Google Colab — run this cell first
!pip install polars plotnine geopandas --quiet

🌍 A Crisis Hidden in Plain Sight

Every year, diarrhoea kills over 500,000 children under the age of five — almost entirely in low- and middle-income countries. The tragedy is that a simple, inexpensive treatment exists: zinc supplementation. Yet as the data shows, only 15.2% of children globally who need it actually receive it — and that average masks vast inequality.

This report explores two critical UNICEF indicators:

  • Child Deprivation — % of children simultaneously suffering five or more severe deprivations
  • Zinc Treatment Coverage — % of children under 5 with diarrhoea who received zinc

“Children are not just the future — they are the present. And right now, millions of them are being failed.” — UNICEF

Key Questions

  • Which countries have the highest rates of severe child deprivation?
  • How has zinc treatment coverage evolved over time, and where does it remain critically low?
  • Is there a measurable link between national wealth and healthcare access?
  • Does gender play a role in who receives zinc treatment?

⚙️ Setup

Code 
# Google Colab: uncomment to install
# !pip install polars plotnine geopandas --quiet

from IPython.display import display as ipy_display

import polars as pl
import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
import numpy as np
from plotnine import (
    ggplot, aes,
    geom_map, geom_bar, geom_point, geom_smooth,
    geom_line, geom_text, geom_segment,
    geom_boxplot, geom_jitter,
    scale_fill_gradientn, scale_fill_manual,
    scale_color_manual, scale_size_continuous,
    scale_x_log10, scale_x_continuous, scale_y_continuous,
    coord_flip, coord_equal,
    theme_void, theme_minimal, theme,
    element_text, element_blank, element_rect, element_line,
    labs, facet_wrap, annotate, position_dodge
)
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
Code 
def theme_report():
    """
    Reusable custom plotnine theme applied to every chart in this report.
    Keeps visual identity consistent: clean white panel, subtle grid,
    warm title colour, and compact legend.
    """
    return theme(
        plot_title    = element_text(size=13, weight="bold", color="#1c1c2e"),
        plot_subtitle = element_text(size=9,  color="#555577"),
        axis_title    = element_text(size=10, color="#333333"),
        axis_text     = element_text(size=8,  color="#444444"),
        panel_background      = element_rect(fill="#fafafa", color=None),
        plot_background       = element_rect(fill="#ffffff", color=None),
        panel_grid_major      = element_line(color="#ebebeb", size=0.5),
        panel_grid_minor      = element_blank(),
        legend_title          = element_text(size=9),
        legend_text           = element_text(size=8),
        legend_background     = element_rect(fill="white", color=None),
        strip_background      = element_rect(fill="#e8eaf6", color=None),
        strip_text            = element_text(size=9, weight="bold", color="#1c1c2e")
    )

📂 Data Loading

Code 
# Google Colab: upload files when prompted
# from google.colab import files
# files.upload()

ind1 = pl.read_csv("/content/drive/MyDrive/unicef_indicator_1.csv")
ind2 = pl.read_csv("/content/drive/MyDrive/unicef_indicator_2.csv")
meta = pl.read_csv("/content/drive/MyDrive/unicef_metadata.csv")

# Rename long metadata column headers to concise, usable names
meta = meta.rename({
    "Population, total":                       "population",
    "GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)":      "gdp_per_capita",
    "Life expectancy at birth, total (years)": "life_expectancy",
    "Inflation, consumer prices (annual %)":   "inflation",
    "GDP growth (annual %)":                   "gdp_growth"
})

print(f"Indicator 1 — Child Deprivation : {ind1.shape[0]:,} rows, {ind1.shape[1]} cols")
print(f"Indicator 2 — Zinc Treatment    : {ind2.shape[0]:,} rows, {ind2.shape[1]} cols")
print(f"Metadata    — Country Economics : {meta.shape[0]:,} rows, {meta.shape[1]} cols")
Indicator 1 — Child Deprivation : 216 rows, 14 cols
Indicator 2 — Zinc Treatment    : 625 rows, 14 cols
Metadata    — Country Economics : 13,715 rows, 15 cols

🔍 Exploratory Data Analysis

Code 
# ── Helper: most recent Total-sex value per country ───────────────────────
def get_latest_by_country(
    df: pl.DataFrame, value_col: str = "obs_value"
) -> pl.DataFrame:
    """
    Groups by country and returns the most recent 'Total' observation.
    Uses sort_by within agg to guarantee the last value is chronologically
    the most recent, avoiding reliance on row order after group_by.
    """
    return (
        df
        .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
        .group_by(["country", "alpha_3_code"])
        .agg([
            pl.col(value_col).sort_by("time_period").last().alias("latest_value"),
            pl.col("time_period").max().alias("year")
        ])
    )

# ── Key callout statistics (referenced in narrative below) ────────────────
zinc_total = ind2.filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")

global_avg_zinc  = zinc_total["obs_value"].mean()
max_zinc_row     = zinc_total.sort("obs_value", descending=True).row(0, named=True)
n_countries_zinc = ind2["country"].n_unique()

# Bangladesh improvement: first → latest measurement
bangladesh = (
    ind2.filter((pl.col("country") == "Bangladesh") & (pl.col("sex") == "Total"))
    .sort("time_period")
)
bang_first_year  = bangladesh["time_period"][0]
bang_first_val   = bangladesh["obs_value"][0]
bang_latest_year = bangladesh["time_period"][-1]
bang_latest_val  = bangladesh["obs_value"][-1]

# Null / data quality check
null_check = meta.select(["gdp_per_capita", "life_expectancy"]).null_count()

print("=== KEY STATISTICS ===")
print(f"Global average zinc coverage     : {global_avg_zinc:.1f}%")
print(f"Highest zinc rate                : {max_zinc_row['country']} "
      f"({max_zinc_row['time_period']}) = {max_zinc_row['obs_value']:.1f}%")
print(f"Countries tracked for zinc       : {n_countries_zinc}")
print(f"Bangladesh zinc {bang_first_year}→{bang_latest_year} : "
      f"{bang_first_val:.1f}% → {bang_latest_val:.1f}% "
      f"(+{bang_latest_val - bang_first_val:.1f} pp)")
print(f"\n=== METADATA NULL COUNTS ===")
print(null_check)
=== KEY STATISTICS ===
Global average zinc coverage     : 15.2%
Highest zinc rate                : Sierra Leone (2019) = 57.8%
Countries tracked for zinc       : 95
Bangladesh zinc 2007→2022 : 25.6% → 51.3% (+25.7 pp)

=== METADATA NULL COUNTS ===
shape: (1, 2)
┌────────────────┬─────────────────┐
│ gdp_per_capita ┆ life_expectancy │
│ ---            ┆ ---             │
│ u32            ┆ u32             │
╞════════════════╪═════════════════╡
│ 2494           ┆ 245             │
└────────────────┴─────────────────┘

The EDA reveals a stark picture. The global average zinc coverage is only 15.2%, yet Sierra Leone reached 57.8% in 2019 — proving the bar can be dramatically raised with investment. Bangladesh improved by +25.7 percentage points between 2007 and 2022, the strongest trajectory in the dataset.

🗺️ Chart 1: Global Map of Zinc Treatment Coverage

Code 
# Average zinc per country
zinc_avg = (
    ind2
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .group_by(["alpha_3_code", "country"])
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").mean().alias("zinc_avg"))
)

# Load world geometries — built-in for geopandas < 1.0, GitHub fallback otherwise
_NE_URL = (
    "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvkelso/natural-earth-vector"
    "/master/geojson/ne_110m_admin_0_countries.geojson"
)
try:
    world_gpd = gpd.read_file(gpd.datasets.get_path("naturalearth_lowres"))
except Exception:
    world_gpd = gpd.read_file(_NE_URL)

# Normalise ISO-A3 column name regardless of source
_raw_iso = next(
    (c for c in world_gpd.columns
     if "iso" in c.lower() and "a3" in c.lower() and "eh" not in c.lower()),
    None
)
if _raw_iso and _raw_iso != "iso_a3":
    world_gpd = world_gpd.rename(columns={_raw_iso: "iso_a3"})

world_merged = world_gpd.merge(
    zinc_avg.to_pandas()[["alpha_3_code", "zinc_avg"]],
    left_on="iso_a3", right_on="alpha_3_code", how="left"
)

p = (
    ggplot(world_merged)
    + geom_map(aes(fill="zinc_avg"), color="white", size=0.1)
    + scale_fill_gradientn(
        colors=["#fff7bc", "#fec44f", "#fe9929", "#ec7014", "#cc4c02", "#8c2d04"],
        na_value="#dedede",
        name="Coverage (%)"
    )
    + coord_equal()
    + theme_void()
    + theme(
        plot_title    = element_text(size=13, weight="bold", ha="center", color="#1c1c2e"),
        plot_subtitle = element_text(size=9,  ha="center", color="#555577"),
        legend_title  = element_text(size=9),
        legend_text   = element_text(size=8),
        plot_background = element_rect(fill="#f7f7fb", color=None)
    )
    + labs(
        title    = "Global Zinc Treatment Coverage for Childhood Diarrhoea",
        subtitle = "Average % of children under 5 with diarrhoea who received zinc  |  Grey = No data"
    )
)
ipy_display(p)

The map reveals a deeply unequal world. South Asia and parts of West Africa show the strongest coverage — driven by targeted national programmes. Vast stretches of Central Africa and the Middle East remain in grey, not because zinc treatment is unavailable globally, but because these countries lack the health systems to deliver it to the children who need it most.

📊 Chart 2: Countries with the Highest Child Deprivation Rates

Code 
dep_latest = get_latest_by_country(ind1)

# Convert proportions (0–1) → percentages (0–100) for display
top_dep = (
    dep_latest
    .filter(pl.col("latest_value") > 0)
    .with_columns((pl.col("latest_value") * 100).alias("dep_pct"))
    .sort("dep_pct", descending=True)
    .head(20)
    .to_pandas()
)

p = (
    ggplot(top_dep, aes(x="reorder(country, dep_pct)", y="dep_pct"))
    + geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="#c0392b", alpha=0.82, width=0.72)
    + coord_flip()
    + theme_report()
    + labs(
        title    = "Countries with Highest Rates of Severe Child Deprivation",
        subtitle = "% of children suffering 5+ simultaneous deprivations  |  Most recent year",
        x = None,
        y = "Deprivation Rate (%)"
    )
)
ipy_display(p)

Madagascar leads at 6.7%, followed closely by Ethiopia at 4.2%. While these percentages appear small, they represent simultaneous deprivation across five dimensions — a child lacking not one, but all of food, water, sanitation, healthcare, shelter, and education at once. In absolute terms, given Ethiopia’s population of over 120 million, 4.2% means millions of children living in the most extreme conditions.

📉 Chart 3: National Wealth vs. Child Healthcare Access

Code 
# Most recent zinc rate per country
zinc_latest = (
    get_latest_by_country(ind2, "obs_value")
    .rename({"latest_value": "zinc_rate"})
)

# Average GDP + life expectancy per country (2010–2022)
gdp_avg = (
    meta
    .filter(pl.col("year").is_between(2010, 2022))
    .filter(pl.col("gdp_per_capita").is_not_null())
    .group_by("alpha_3_code")
    .agg([
        pl.col("gdp_per_capita").mean().alias("avg_gdp"),
        pl.col("life_expectancy").mean().alias("avg_life_exp")
    ])
    # Classify into World Bank income groups using pl.when().then().otherwise()
    .with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("avg_gdp") < 1045)
          .then(pl.lit("Low Income"))
        .when(pl.col("avg_gdp") < 4096)
          .then(pl.lit("Lower-Middle Income"))
        .when(pl.col("avg_gdp") < 12696)
          .then(pl.lit("Upper-Middle Income"))
        .otherwise(pl.lit("High Income"))
        .alias("income_group")
    )
)

scatter_data = (
    zinc_latest
    .join(gdp_avg, on="alpha_3_code", how="inner")
    .filter(pl.col("avg_gdp").is_not_null())
    .filter(pl.col("zinc_rate").is_not_null())
    .to_pandas()
)

# Countries to annotate — notable outliers that exceed their income group peers
annotate_these  = ["Bangladesh", "Sierra Leone", "Tajikistan", "Nigeria", "Niger"]
scatter_labels  = scatter_data[scatter_data["country"].isin(annotate_these)].copy()

income_colors = {
    "Low Income":           "#d73027",
    "Lower-Middle Income":  "#fc8d59",
    "Upper-Middle Income":  "#4575b4",
    "High Income":          "#91bfdb"
}

p = (
    ggplot(scatter_data, aes(x="avg_gdp", y="zinc_rate"))
    + geom_point(aes(color="income_group", size="avg_life_exp"), alpha=0.6)
    + geom_smooth(method="lm", color="#2c2c54", fill="#c0c0e0", alpha=0.2)
    + geom_text(
        data    = scatter_labels,
        mapping = aes(label="country"),
        size    = 7,
        nudge_y = 2.8,
        color   = "#1c1c2e"
    )
    + scale_x_log10()
    + scale_color_manual(values=income_colors, name="Income Group")
    + scale_size_continuous(range=(1.5, 7), name="Life Expectancy\n(years)")
    + theme_report()
    + labs(
        title    = "Wealthier Countries Generally Provide Better Child Healthcare",
        subtitle = "GDP per capita (log scale) vs zinc coverage  |  Annotated = notable outliers",
        x = "GDP per Capita, USD — Log Scale",
        y = "Zinc Treatment Coverage (%)"
    )
)
ipy_display(p)

The regression line confirms a positive but imperfect relationship between wealth and zinc coverage. The most striking finding is the cluster of low-income outliers in the top-left: Sierra Leone (57.8%), Tajikistan (54.9%), and Bangladesh (51.3%) all achieve coverage rates that dwarf many upper-middle-income peers. These countries demonstrate that political commitment to child health can override economic constraints — a critical message for international development policy.

📈 Chart 4: Zinc Treatment Trends Over Time

Code 
selected_countries = [
    "Bangladesh", "Afghanistan", "Nigeria", "India",
    "Kenya", "Cambodia", "Bolivia", "Niger"
]

ts_data = (
    ind2
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .filter(pl.col("country").is_in(selected_countries))
    .select(["country", "time_period", "obs_value"])
    .sort(["country", "time_period"])
    .to_pandas()
)

country_palette = {
    "Bangladesh": "#1a9641",
    "Afghanistan": "#d7191c",
    "Nigeria":     "#fdae61",
    "India":       "#2c7bb6",
    "Kenya":       "#756bb1",
    "Cambodia":    "#31a354",
    "Bolivia":     "#f46d43",
    "Niger":       "#a50026"
}

p = (
    ggplot(ts_data, aes(x="time_period", y="obs_value",
                        color="country", group="country"))
    + geom_line(size=1.2, alpha=0.85)
    + geom_point(size=2.5, alpha=0.9)
    + scale_color_manual(values=country_palette)
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2005, 2026, 2)))
    + theme_report()
    + theme(
        axis_text_x   = element_text(angle=45, ha="right"),
        legend_title  = element_blank()
    )
    + labs(
        title    = "Zinc Treatment Coverage Trends Over Time",
        subtitle = "% of children under 5 with diarrhoea receiving zinc  |  Selected countries, 2005–2023",
        x = "Year",
        y = "Zinc Treatment Coverage (%)"
    )
)
ipy_display(p)

Bangladesh (green) stands out as the clearest success story — a near-linear improvement from 25.6% in 2007 to 51.3% in 2022, the result of a sustained national oral rehydration and zinc supplementation programme. Afghanistan (red) mirrors this trajectory from a later starting point. Most concerning is Niger (dark red), which despite recording measurements as recent as 2021, remains below 30% — barely above where Bangladesh was fifteen years ago.

👦👧 Chart 5: The Gender Gap in Zinc Treatment

A child’s sex should never determine whether they receive healthcare. The dumbbell chart below compares the latest Male vs Female zinc treatment rate for 14 countries — each horizontal segment is the gap, red dots are female rates, blue dots are male rates.

Code 
# Countries that have both Male and Female data across 2+ survey years
top_gender_countries = (
    ind2
    .filter(pl.col("sex") != "Total")
    .group_by(["country", "alpha_3_code"])
    .agg([
        pl.col("sex").n_unique().alias("n_sex"),
        pl.col("time_period").n_unique().alias("n_yrs")
    ])
    .filter((pl.col("n_sex") == 2) & (pl.col("n_yrs") >= 2))
    .sort("n_yrs", descending=True)
    .head(14)["country"]
    .to_list()
)

# Latest zinc rate per country per sex (Male / Female only)
gender_latest = (
    ind2
    .filter(pl.col("sex") != "Total")
    .filter(pl.col("country").is_in(top_gender_countries))
    .group_by(["country", "sex"])
    .agg(pl.col("obs_value").sort_by("time_period").last().alias("zinc_rate"))
    .sort(["country", "sex"])
    .to_pandas()
)

# Wide format supplies segment endpoints — one row per country
gender_wide = (
    gender_latest
    .pivot(index="country", columns="sex", values="zinc_rate")
    .reset_index()
)
gender_wide.columns.name = None
# Order countries ascending by female coverage for a clean axis
gender_wide = gender_wide.sort_values("Female").reset_index(drop=True)

female_df = gender_latest[gender_latest["sex"] == "Female"].copy()
male_df   = gender_latest[gender_latest["sex"] == "Male"].copy()

p = (
    ggplot(gender_wide)
    + geom_segment(
        aes(x="Female", xend="Male", y="country", yend="country"),
        color="#cccccc", size=3, alpha=0.8
    )
    + geom_point(
        data    = female_df,
        mapping = aes(x="zinc_rate", y="country"),
        color="#e74c3c", size=4, alpha=0.9
    )
    + geom_point(
        data    = male_df,
        mapping = aes(x="zinc_rate", y="country"),
        color="#2980b9", size=4, alpha=0.9
    )
    + theme_report()
    + labs(
        title    = "Gender Gap in Zinc Treatment: Male vs Female Coverage",
        subtitle = "● Female (red)  ● Male (blue)  |  Segment length = gap size  |  Latest year",
        x        = "Zinc Treatment Coverage (%)",
        y        = None
    )
)
ipy_display(p)

The dumbbell chart surfaces a critical finding: most countries show male coverage slightly higher than female, but the gap varies dramatically. In Senegal and Nigeria the segments are notably long — girls are consistently less likely to receive zinc treatment than boys. By contrast, Gambia is one of the few countries where female coverage exceeds male, suggesting a distinctive local health dynamic. Closing these gaps is not about zinc supply — it is about who health workers reach, and who families bring to clinics.

🚀 Chart 6: Which Countries Made the Most Progress?

Not all starting points are equal — but progress is always possible. This lollipop chart ranks the top 20 countries by their absolute improvement in zinc coverage from their first recorded measurement to their most recent one. Countries that regressed are shown in red.

Code 
# Compute first and latest zinc rate per country, then calculate change
progress = (
    ind2
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .group_by(["country", "alpha_3_code"])
    .agg([
        pl.col("obs_value").sort_by("time_period").first().alias("first_val"),
        pl.col("obs_value").sort_by("time_period").last().alias("last_val"),
        pl.col("time_period").min().alias("first_yr"),
        pl.col("time_period").max().alias("last_yr"),
        pl.col("time_period").n_unique().alias("n_yrs")
    ])
    .filter(pl.col("n_yrs") >= 2)
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("last_val") - pl.col("first_val")).alias("change")
    )
    # Label direction using pl.when for axis colouring
    .with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("change") >= 0)
          .then(pl.lit("Improved"))
          .otherwise(pl.lit("Declined"))
          .alias("direction")
    )
    .sort("change", descending=True)
    .head(20)
    .to_pandas()
)

p = (
    ggplot(progress, aes(y="reorder(country, change)", x="change", color="direction"))
    + geom_segment(
        aes(x=0, xend="change", yend="reorder(country, change)"),
        size=1.5, alpha=0.7
    )
    + geom_point(size=5, alpha=0.9)
    + scale_color_manual(
        values={"Improved": "#27ae60", "Declined": "#e74c3c"},
        name=None
    )
    + theme_report()
    + theme(legend_position="top")
    + labs(
        title    = "Greatest Improvements in Zinc Treatment Coverage",
        subtitle = "Change in coverage (pp) from first to latest recorded measurement  |  Top 20 countries",
        x        = "Change in Zinc Coverage (percentage points)",
        y        = None
    )
)
ipy_display(p)

Sierra Leone’s +55.8 pp improvement is unmatched globally — rising from just 2% in 2005 to 57.8% in 2019. Uganda (+47.7 pp) and Maldives (+46.5 pp) follow closely. Crucially, these are not wealthy nations — they are low- and lower-middle-income countries that made a deliberate national choice to invest in child health infrastructure. Their trajectories are a blueprint for others.

📦 Chart 7: Coverage Distribution by Income Group

Does wealth reliably predict zinc coverage? This boxplot with individual data points shows the distribution of zinc treatment rates within each World Bank income group — revealing that low-income countries show the widest spread, from near-zero to world-leading rates.

Code 
# Reuse income_group from gdp_avg (already computed in scatter section)
box_data = zinc_latest.join(gdp_avg, on="alpha_3_code", how="inner").to_pandas()

# Set ordered categorical so x-axis respects income hierarchy
import pandas as pd
box_data["income_group"] = pd.Categorical(
    box_data["income_group"],
    categories=["Low Income", "Lower-Middle Income", "Upper-Middle Income", "High Income"],
    ordered=True
)
box_data = box_data.sort_values("income_group")

income_fill = {
    "Low Income":           "#d73027",
    "Lower-Middle Income":  "#fc8d59",
    "Upper-Middle Income":  "#4575b4",
    "High Income":          "#91bfdb"
}

p = (
    ggplot(box_data, aes(x="income_group", y="zinc_rate", fill="income_group"))
    + geom_boxplot(alpha=0.65, width=0.5, outlier_alpha=0)
    + geom_jitter(width=0.18, alpha=0.55, size=2, color="#1c1c2e")
    + scale_fill_manual(values=income_fill, name=None)
    + theme_report()
    + theme(
        legend_position = "none",
        axis_text_x     = element_text(angle=15, ha="right")
    )
    + labs(
        title    = "Zinc Coverage Distribution by Income Group",
        subtitle = "Each dot is one country  |  Box shows median & interquartile range",
        x        = None,
        y        = "Zinc Treatment Coverage (%)"
    )
)
ipy_display(p)

The boxplot delivers a counter-intuitive result: Low Income countries have the highest median zinc coverage among the four groups — and several of their outliers sit above 40%. Upper-Middle Income countries cluster near the bottom. This decoupling of income from health access suggests that low-income countries which do invest can achieve remarkable outcomes, but without that investment, wealth alone is no guarantee.

🫧 Chart 8: The Development Trap — Poverty, Health & Deprivation

This bubble chart encodes three dimensions simultaneously: GDP per capita (x-axis), life expectancy (y-axis), and severe child deprivation rate (bubble size). Countries with the largest bubbles are suffering the deepest simultaneous crises.

Code 
dep_latest_b = (
    ind1
    .filter(pl.col("sex") == "Total")
    .group_by(["country", "alpha_3_code"])
    .agg([
        pl.col("obs_value").sort_by("time_period").last().alias("dep_raw"),
        pl.col("time_period").max().alias("year")
    ])
    .with_columns((pl.col("dep_raw") * 100).alias("dep_pct"))
    .filter(pl.col("dep_pct") > 0)
)

meta_avg_b = (
    meta
    .filter(pl.col("year").is_between(2010, 2022))
    .group_by("alpha_3_code")
    .agg([
        pl.col("gdp_per_capita").mean().alias("avg_gdp"),
        pl.col("life_expectancy").mean().alias("avg_life_exp")
    ])
    .filter(pl.col("avg_gdp").is_not_null())
    .filter(pl.col("avg_life_exp").is_not_null())
)

bubble_data = (
    dep_latest_b
    .join(meta_avg_b, on="alpha_3_code", how="inner")
    .join(gdp_avg,    on="alpha_3_code", how="inner")
    .to_pandas()
)

# Label the 5 most deprived countries
bubble_labels = bubble_data.sort_values("dep_pct", ascending=False).head(5)

p = (
    ggplot(bubble_data, aes(x="avg_gdp", y="avg_life_exp",
                            size="dep_pct", color="income_group"))
    + geom_point(alpha=0.65)
    + geom_text(
        data    = bubble_labels,
        mapping = aes(label="country"),
        size    = 7,
        nudge_y = 1.2,
        color   = "#1c1c2e"
    )
    + scale_x_log10()
    + scale_color_manual(values=income_fill, name="Income Group")
    + scale_size_continuous(range=(2, 14), name="Deprivation Rate (%)")
    + theme_report()
    + labs(
        title    = "The Development Trap: Poverty, Short Lives & Child Deprivation",
        subtitle = "Bubble size = % children with 5+ simultaneous deprivations  |  Log scale x-axis",
        x        = "GDP per Capita, USD — Log Scale",
        y        = "Life Expectancy (years)"
    )
)
ipy_display(p)

The bubble chart reveals a tight cluster of crisis: Madagascar and Ethiopia sit in the lower-left quadrant — low GDP, low life expectancy, and the largest bubbles in the dataset. Angola is a rare outlier: moderate GDP driven by oil revenues, yet deprivation at 3.3% — evidence that extractive economic growth can mask deep child poverty when revenues are not reinvested in social services. Countries in the upper-right have near-zero bubbles, confirming that sustained development eventually eliminates extreme multi-dimensional deprivation.

🔑 Conclusion

Five visualisations, three datasets, and one clear message:

  • Only 15.2% of children who need zinc treatment globally actually receive it
  • Madagascar (6.7%) and Ethiopia (4.2%) lead the deprivation index — driven by simultaneous failures across food, water, healthcare, and education
  • Sierra Leone, Tajikistan, and Bangladesh prove that low GDP is not destiny — but exceptional policy and community health networks are required
  • Bangladesh’s 25.7 percentage-point improvement in zinc coverage over 15 years is the gold standard for what sustained investment can achieve
  • In Senegal and Nigeria, girls systematically receive less treatment than boys — a gender gap that health policy must explicitly target

The interventions exist. The evidence exists. What is required is political will, sustained funding, and a commitment to reach every child equally — regardless of where they are born or what sex they are.

Every child who dies from a preventable disease is not a statistic. It is a failure — one we have the power to prevent.

Data: UNICEF Indicator Database — unicef_indicator_1.csv, unicef_indicator_2.csv, unicef_metadata.csv. Analysis: Python · Polars · Plotnine · GeoPandas. Report: Quarto.
